title: Cat Excessive Urination: Why Your Cat is Flooding the Litter Box
meta_desc: Is your cat flooding the litter box? Learn about polyuria, common causes like diabetes, and when to contact your vet for an appointment.
content_html:
TL;DR: Excessive urination (polyuria) in cats is often a sign of underlying conditions like diabetes or kidney disease and generally requires a veterinary evaluation within 24 to 48 hours.

What does it mean if my cat is urinating excessively?

Excessive urination, medically referred to as polyuria, is a condition where a cat produces an abnormally large volume of urine. You may notice your cat spending more time in the litter box or finding unusually large, heavy clumps that seem to "flood" the litter. This is often a sign that the body is unable to concentrate urine properly or is trying to flush out toxins.

Is my cat’s excessive urination a veterinary emergency?

  • While polyuria is generally a medium-urgency concern, it is a serious clinical sign of underlying illnesses such as diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, or hyperthyroidism.
  • You should aim to have your cat evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Contact an emergency clinic immediately if your cat is also lethargic, vomiting, or has a complete loss of appetite.

How can taking a photo of the litter box help my veterinarian?

  • Visual evidence of the size and frequency of urine clumps provides a precise scale of volume for the veterinary team.
  • Photos of the urine's color or consistency help the vet differentiate between polyuria and other conditions like hematuria (blood in the urine).

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Excessive urination in cats can stem from various underlying conditions. Feline lower urinary tract disease (FLUTD) encompasses several associated conditions, including urinary tract infection, neoplasia, trauma, urethral plugs, urolithiasis, and sterile cystitis (feline interstitial cystitis). Urolithiasis, with stones composed of calcium oxalate, is increasingly prevalent. Disorders of micturition (e.g., urethral incompetence), anatomic abnormalities (e.g., hooded vulva, patent urachus, ectopic ureters, uroliths), and concurrent diseases (e.g., chronic kidney disease, hyperadrenocorticism, chronic glucocorticoid administration) can predispose cats to urinary issues. Diagnostic procedures may include abdominal radiographs, ultrasonography, cystoscopy, and/or double-contrast cystourethrography to exclude radiolucent urocystoliths, anatomic defects, polyps, and neoplasia. A serum biochemical profile, CBC, and complete urinalysis are important to exclude predisposing systemic diseases, such as chronic kidney disease, hyperadrenocorticism, and diabetes mellitus. Further diagnostic considerations include feline immunodeficiency virus, feline leukemia virus, and hyperthyroidism.

Chapter: Neurology, Urology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1524)

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of excessive urination?
faqs.answer: The most frequent causes in cats include feline diabetes, chronic kidney disease, hyperthyroidism, and occasionally urinary tract infections or liver issues.

faqs.question: Should I limit my cat's water if they are urinating too much?
faqs.answer: No, you should never restrict your cat's access to water. Increased urination usually causes dehydration, and your cat drinks more to compensate. Restricting water can lead to a dangerous medical crisis.

faqs.question: What tests will the vet perform?
faqs.answer: Your vet will likely recommend a urinalysis to check concentration and glucose levels, as well as blood work to evaluate kidney function, blood sugar, and thyroid levels.
